title: Charmed Life - Chapter 1 (Fragment 3)
text:
Gwendolen put out her hand for them at the same moment. She did not say anything. Neither did Mrs. Sharp. Both their hands stood still in the air. There was a feeling of fierce invisible struggle. Then Mrs. Sharp took her hand away. “Thank you,” Gwendolen said coldly, and put the earrings away in the pocket of her black dress.

“You see what I mean?” Mrs. Sharp said, making the best of it. “You have real talent, dearie!” She went back to sorting the other things in the box. She turned over an old pipe, ribbons, a spray of white heather, menus, concert tickets, and picked up a bundle of old letters. She ran her thumb down the edge of it. “Love letters,” she said. “His to her.” She put the bundle down without looking at it and picked up another. “Hers to him. No use.” Cat, watching Mrs. Sharp’s broad mauve thumb whirring down a third bundle of letters, thought that being a witch must save a great deal of time. “Business letters,” said Mrs. Sharp. Her thumb paused, and went slowly back up the pile again. “Now what have we here?” she said. She untied the pink tape from around the bundle and carefully took out three letters. She unfolded them.

“Chrestomanci!” she exclaimed. And, as soon as she said it, she clapped one hand over her mouth and mumbled behind it. Her face was red. Cat could see she was surprised, frightened, and greedy, all at the same time. “Now what was he doing writing to your pa?” she said, as soon as she had recovered.

“Let’s see,” said Gwendolen.

Mrs. Sharp spread the three letters out on the kitchen table, and Gwendolen and Cat bent over them. The first thing that struck Cat was the energy of the signature on all three:

The next thing he saw was that two of the letters were written in the same energetic writing as the signature. The first was dated twelve years ago, soon after his parents had been married. It said:

Dear Frank,

Now don’t get on your high horse. I only offered because I thought it might help. I still will help, in any way I can, if you let me know what I can do. I feel you have a claim on me.

Yrs ever,

Chrestomanci

The second letter was shorter:

Dear Chant,

The same to you. Go to blazes.

Chrestomanci

The third letter was dated six years ago, and it was written by someone else. Chrestomanci had only signed it.

Sir,

You were warned six years ago that something like what you relate might come to pass, and you made it quite clear that you wished for no help from this quarter. We are not interested in your troubles. Nor is this a charitable institution.

Chrestomanci

“What did your pa say to him?” Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck. “Well—what do you think, dearie?”

Gwendolen held her hands spread out above the letters, rather as if she was warming them at a fire. Both her little fingers twitched. “I don’t know. They feel important—especially the first one and the last one—awfully important.”

“Who’s Chrestomanci?” Cat asked. It was a hard name to say. He said it in pieces, trying to remember the way Mrs. Sharp had said it: KREST—OH—MAN—SEE. “Is that the right way?”

“Yes, that’s right—and never you mind who he is, my love,” said Mrs. Sharp. “And important’s a weak word for it, dearie. I wish I knew what your pa had said. Something not many people’d dare say, by the sound of it. And look what he got in return! Three genuine signatures! Mr. Nostrum would give his eyes for those, dearie. Oh, you’re in luck! He’ll teach you for those all right! So would any necromancer in the country.”

vocabulary.definition: Filled with feelings of awe or wonder.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp wondered, curious and awestruck.

vocabulary.definition: Used as an emphatic in phrases like 'go to blazes' meaning 'go away' or 'go to hell'.
vocabulary.example: Go to blazes.

vocabulary.definition: A collection of things wrapped or tied together.
vocabulary.example: She picked up a bundle of old letters.

vocabulary.definition: Relating to the assistance of those in need; benevolent.
vocabulary.example: Nor is this a charitable institution.

vocabulary.definition: A demand for something as rightful or due.
vocabulary.example: I feel you have a claim on me.

vocabulary.word: clapped one hand over her mouth
vocabulary.lemma: clapped one hand over her mouth
vocabulary.definition: Used to indicate an attempt to muffle a sound or a sudden reaction to surprise or shock.
vocabulary.example: she clapped one hand over her mouth and mumbled behind it.

vocabulary.definition: In an unemotional or unfriendly manner.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen said coldly.

vocabulary.definition: To have the courage to do something.
vocabulary.example: Something not many people’d dare say.

vocabulary.definition: Pieces of jewellery worn on the earlobes.
vocabulary.example: She put the earrings away in the pocket of her black dress.

vocabulary.definition: Showing or involving great activity or vitality.
vocabulary.example: The energy of the signature on all three letters was striking.

vocabulary.definition: Having or displaying an intense or ferocious aggressiveness.
vocabulary.example: There was a feeling of fierce invisible struggle.

vocabulary.definition: Truly what something is said to be; authentic.
vocabulary.example: Three genuine signatures.

vocabulary.definition: Having an intense desire for something, especially more than is needed.
vocabulary.example: she was surprised, frightened, and greedy, all at the same time.

vocabulary.definition: A low-growing shrub with small purple flowers, common on Scottish moorland.
vocabulary.example: a spray of white heather

vocabulary.definition: An established organisation, especially one dedicated to public service.
vocabulary.example: a charitable institution

vocabulary.definition: A conflict or fight that cannot be seen, often metaphorical.
vocabulary.example: There was a feeling of fierce invisible struggle.

vocabulary.definition: To speak quietly and indistinctly, making it difficult to hear.
vocabulary.example: she clapped one hand over her mouth and mumbled behind it.

vocabulary.definition: The correct way to pronounce a proper name.
vocabulary.example: He said it in pieces, trying to remember the way Mrs. Sharp had said it: KREST—OH—MAN—SEE.

vocabulary.definition: A person who practices necromancy; a sorcerer who communicates with the dead.
vocabulary.example: So would any necromancer in the country.

vocabulary.definition: Long narrow strips of fabric used for tying or decoration.
vocabulary.example: She turned over an old pipe, ribbons, a spray of white heather, menus, concert tickets.

vocabulary.definition: A person's name written in a distinctive way as a form of identification.
vocabulary.example: the energy of the signature on all three

vocabulary.definition: The action of arranging items into groups or categories.
vocabulary.example: She went back to sorting the other things in the box.

vocabulary.definition: To arrange something over a surface so it covers it.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp spread the three letters out on the kitchen table.

vocabulary.definition: A natural aptitude or skill.
vocabulary.example: You have real talent, dearie!

vocabulary.definition: To make a small, sudden jerking movement.
vocabulary.example: Both her little fingers twitched.
